description:
You are an edge-computing engineer about to push a hot-fix to a fleet of IoT gateways.  
    Before proceeding you must prove that a simple one-liner regular-expression filter you wrote
    can correctly extract every “high-temperature” error from yesterday’s raw sensor log.

    1. A log file already exists at  
       /home/user/iot_deploy/logs/sensor.log  

    2. Each line of this file follows exactly this layout (one ASCII space between every token):  
       YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ <LEVEL> <device_id> TEMP=<floating-point-Celsius> HUM=<integer-percent>  

       Example:  
       2023-07-01T10:00:00Z INFO  device123 TEMP=75.2 HUM=40

    3. “High temperature” is defined as both of these conditions being true at the same time:  
       • the log-level is the literal word ERROR  
       • the temperature value begins with either 8 or 9 (that is, TEMP=8xx.x or TEMP=9xx.x)

    4. Create a new file at exactly  
       /home/user/iot_deploy/logs/high_temp_errors.log  

       The file must contain only those lines from sensor.log that satisfy the two conditions
       in the exact same order they originally appeared.  
       • No leading or trailing blank lines.  
       • Lines must be separated by a single Unix newline (<LF>, byte 0x0A).  
       • The file must end with exactly one newline.  
       • Absolutely no extra characters, colour codes, headers or footers.

    5. You must accomplish the extraction with a single terminal command that relies on a POSIX-compatible
       regular expression utility (grep, egrep, awk, sed etc. are all acceptable).  
       Do NOT open an interactive editor; do not modify the original sensor.log.  
       When you’re done, the new file has to exist on disk with the precise contents described
       above so the automated test can run a byte-for-byte comparison.

    6. For human verification you may print the resulting file to the console,
       but make sure it is also written to the correct path because the grader will
       only look at the file itself.

    Goal recap (what the grader will check):
       a. The file /home/user/iot_deploy/logs/high_temp_errors.log exists.  
       b. It contains exactly the three qualifying lines and nothing else.  
       c. Line order matches their appearance order in sensor.log.  
       d. File ends with a single trailing newline.  
       e. Sensor.log remains unchanged.

    Hints:  
       • Remember that basic grep treats ‘.’ as any character; escape literal dots if needed.  
       • You can capture the “temperature begins with 8 or 9” rule directly in the regex.  
       • Use redirection (> or >>) to create/write the output file in a single command invocation.
truth:
Initial setup (already present when the task begins):

    Directory:
      /home/user/iot_deploy/logs            (permissions 755, owned by user)

    File:
      /home/user/iot_deploy/logs/sensor.log (permissions 644, owned by user)

    Exact byte-for-byte contents of sensor.log (every line terminated by a single LF):

        2023-07-01T10:00:00Z INFO device123 TEMP=75.2 HUM=40
        2023-07-01T10:05:00Z ERROR device123 TEMP=82.5 HUM=42
        2023-07-01T10:10:00Z WARN device456 TEMP=60.1 HUM=50
        2023-07-01T10:15:00Z ERROR device789 TEMP=90.0 HUM=35
        2023-07-01T10:20:00Z INFO device123 TEMP=78.3 HUM=45
        2023-07-01T10:25:00Z ERROR device456 TEMP=87.4 HUM=47
        2023-07-01T10:30:00Z INFO device789 TEMP=72.0 HUM=38
      (Note: 7 lines total, each including the trailing newline shown above.)

    Desired end state after the agent finishes:

    A new file must exist:
      /home/user/iot_deploy/logs/high_temp_errors.log (permissions 644, owned by user)

    Exact required contents of high_temp_errors.log (again, each line terminated by exactly one LF):

        2023-07-01T10:05:00Z ERROR device123 TEMP=82.5 HUM=42
        2023-07-01T10:15:00Z ERROR device789 TEMP=90.0 HUM=35
        2023-07-01T10:25:00Z ERROR device456 TEMP=87.4 HUM=47

    No other files should be changed or created inside /home/user/iot_deploy/logs.
    The grader will fail the task if:
       • sensor.log is missing or altered,
       • high_temp_errors.log is missing, has extra/ missing characters, wrong line order,
         additional blank lines, or lacks the final newline,
       • any additional files are created in the directory,
       • file permissions differ from the defaults noted above.
